title: Build the Point Step by Step

root.narration: You are rallying from the baseline and your opponent is defending well. Every shot comes back. You need a plan to open up the court. What sequence of shots do you use?

choices.text: Hit two deep crosscourt forehands to move your opponent wide, then when they hit a short ball, attack it down the line to the open court
Hit every ball to the same spot and hope your opponent makes an error
Go for a huge winner on the very first ball even though your opponent is in perfect position

outcome.headline: Perfectly constructed point!
outcome.explanation: By going crosscourt twice, you moved your opponent further and further to one side. When they finally hit a short ball, the down-the-line winner was wide open. That is how you build a point.
outcome.whatToRemember: Great tennis is like chess. Use crosscourt shots to move your opponent, then attack down the line when an opening appears. Patience plus a plan beats raw power.

keyTerms.definition: Building a point by using a planned sequence of shots to move your opponent around the court, creating an opening for a winner rather than going for one right away

outcome.headline: Predictable pattern!
outcome.explanation: Hitting to the same spot over and over let your opponent camp there and hit comfortable returns. They eventually found an opening and moved you around instead.
outcome.whatToRemember: Vary your shots to move your opponent. If every ball goes to the same place, your opponent knows exactly where to stand.

outcome.headline: Unforced error!
outcome.explanation: Going for a winner when your opponent is perfectly positioned and the ball is deep is a low-percentage play. Your shot clipped the net cord and dropped short on your side.
outcome.whatToRemember: Do not force winners from bad positions. Build the point first and wait for the right ball to attack.
